coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots as an emergent antibacterial agent
coredata.dc:creator: Tariq, Mohammad
Singh, Anirudh
Varshney, Neelima
Samanta, Sintu Kumar
Sk, Md Palashuddin
coredata.dc:description: The biomass-derived Cdots have several advantages over chemically routed synthesized Cdots for promising biological applications. Biomass-derived Cdots are synthesized from medicinally important natural products, suitable for sustainable applications, and biomass offers an excellent carbon source. The bacterial multi-drug resistance has become a serious threat to human health. Hence, the development of biomass-derived Cdots as potential antibacterial agents could be an alternative to presently available antibiotics (or metallodrugs and metal nanoparticles) in primary and secondary care. Herein, we report a rapid microwave-mediated synthesis method of figs-derived (Ficus religiosa tree) red emissive Cdots as a bactericidal agent. The formation of figs-derived Cdots was characterized using various techniques such as Uv-visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, TRPL, FTIR, TEM, and XPS. The figs-derived Cdots showed superior antibacterial activity against ampicillin-resistant Gram-negative strain of E. coli (DH5α) and tetracyclin-resistant Gram-positive strain of B. subtilis (MTCC 121) than the figs extracted product which has inherent antibacterial activity. The NBT assay revealed that the bacterial cell death might be due to ROS-mediated, which was further confirmed by the SEM study. This study might provide an opportunity to develop biomass-derived Cdots as a novel antibacterial agent to treat drug-resistant bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Antibacterial
Antimicrobial resistance
Biocompatibility
Sustainable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of oxidative stress through NBT assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Indian Institute of Information Technology, Allahabad
Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: MPS, MT, and NV acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University (AMU), for providing instrumental facilities. The authors acknowledge University Sophisticated Instrument Facility, AMU, Aligarh, and MNIT Jaipur for XPS facility. AS and SKS acknowledge the Department of Applied Sciences, IIIT Allahabad, for providing the facility. We thank Dr.Amaresh Kumar Sahoo (IIIT Allahabad) and. M. S. Hassan (IIT Delhi) for their help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of figs-derived Cdots dispersed in ethanol (0.032 mg/mL). b) Excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of Cdots (0.1 mg/mL) in ethanol, excited from 360–450 nm. c) represents the excitation spectrum of Cdots, which was recorded for the emission wavelength maximum of 673 nm. d) Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) plot of the Cdots (excited with a 375 nm laser source), which denotes the single exponential decay profile for Cdots.
a) TEM analysis indicates the formation of Cdots. Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s signal (b), O1s signal (c), and Si2p signal (d).
a) Growth curve of E. coli (DH5α) treated with and without Cdots and Figs extracted product at MIC concentration. b) NBT assay of Cdots treated E. coli (DH5α) at MIC and half MIC concentration.
SEM images of E.coli bacteria with different treatments. a) untreated control samples and b) figs-derived Cdots treated samples.
MIC and MBC value of C-dots against E. coli (DH5α).

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots as an emergent antibacterial agent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh-202002, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad, Allahabad 211012, Uttar Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Sciences, Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad, Allahabad-211012, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The biomass-derived Cdots have several advantages over chemically routed synthesized Cdots for promising biological applications. Biomass-derived Cdots are synthesized from medicinally important natural products, suitable for sustainable applications, and biomass offers an excellent carbon source. The bacterial multi-drug resistance has become a serious threat to human health. Hence, the development of biomass-derived Cdots as potential antibacterial agents could be an alternative to presently available antibiotics (or metallodrugs and metal nanoparticles) in primary and secondary care. Herein, we report a rapid microwave-mediated synthesis method of figs-derived (Ficus religiosa tree) red emissive Cdots as a bactericidal agent. The formation of figs-derived Cdots was characterized using various techniques such as Uv-visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, TRPL, FTIR, TEM, and XPS. The figs-derived Cdots showed superior antibacterial activity against ampicillin-resistant Gram-negative strain of E. coli (DH5α) and tetracyclin-resistant Gram-positive strain of B. subtilis (MTCC 121) than the figs extracted product which has inherent antibacterial activity. The NBT assay revealed that the bacterial cell death might be due to ROS-mediated, which was further confirmed by the SEM study. This study might provide an opportunity to develop biomass-derived Cdots as a novel antibacterial agent to treat drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data is included in the manuscript and supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of antibiotics can be considered as one of the most outstanding and notable achievements in medical science. These have become instrumental in the treatment of numerous bacterial infections. Unfortunately, the development of antibiotic resistance in bacteria has become a global health issue due to the misuse and rampant use of antibiotics [1]. It has been estimated that a rapid increase in bacterial infection may be caused more than 10 million fatalities annually by 2050 world wide [2]. Treating various drug-resistant bacterial infections has become challenging since conventional antibiotics have become less effective or ineffective. Such a dramatic increase in bacterial resistance to antibiotics is now threatening therapeutic achievement. This health issue has become more alarming, particularly in third-world countries, where the usage of antibiotics is not monitored appropriately. It results in multi-drug resistant (MDR) and extensive drug-resistant (XDR) bacterial strains. The ESKAPE (E. faecium, S. aureus, K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, P. aeruginosa, and Enterobacterspp) group of pathogens can easily evade commonly used antibiotics and consequently have become the major cause of life-threatening nosocomial infections throughout the world. Therefore, the emergence of drug-resistant bacterial strains followed by their rapid spread worldwide has become a serious global health concern. It has intensified the concern of the whole medical fraternity to urgently develop therapeutics with broad-spectrum antibacterial potency with novel targets and a new approach to replace conventional antibiotics effectively.

The human body's primary barrier against any external threats is skin. Although, it readily damages and produces wounds in daily life due to this injured area of the skin-transforming into a sore [3]. To resolve this problem, some antibacterial agents have been used in past decades, such as chloramphenicol, trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, β-lactam, etc. However, many organisms have developed resistance to these drugs. The failure of these antibiotics to treat bacterial infection will lengthen the time of staying of patients in hospital, enhancing the healthcare cost and death rate [2]. So, the search for novel material with superior characteristics and specificity is needed to combat these infections. The majority of novel antibiotics are created through functional and structural modifications. However, the synthesis of these drugs is gradual, but the growth of drug-resistant bacteria is rapid [4]. In this context, nanoscale materials can be considered effective alternatives due to their physicochemical properties and unique dimensions. Various nanomaterials (metal nanostructure, semiconducting quantum dots, non-metallic quantum dots, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotube) with intrinsic biological properties have been synthesized as nanomedicine [5–9]. The distinct metal nanostructure/semiconducting quantum dots exhibit antibacterial activities, but overuse of these metal nanostructure/semiconducting quantum dots shows the cytotoxicity that limits their practical application [10–13]. Therefore, biocompatible carbon dots (Cdots) have been emerging as promising antibacterial agents [3,5,11,14,15].

Cdots, a new class of fluorescent nanostructure of the carbon family, have several advantages over other antibacterial agents because of their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, chemical stability, high photostability, exciting optical properties, ease of functionalization, etc. [16] The synthesis methods, which involve polymerization of carbonaceous precursors followed by carbonization, are less complicated and cost-effective. However, biomass-derived synthesis of Cdots is more environmentally friendly, preferable for large-scale synthesis, inexpensive, and suitable for divergent sustainable applications. [17,32] These biomass-derived Cdots are popularly known for their promising photoluminescence-based applications, but their antibacterial study needs to be explored. [32,33] Although, Cdots, synthesized using chemicals & reagents, have shown antibacterial effects with great potential. Herein, we develop a microwave-mediated synthesis of Cdots from the fig of the Ficusreligiosa plant and further study their antibacterial activity. We have employed the ampicillin-resistant Gram-negative strain of E. coli (DH5α) and tetracyclin-resistant Gram-positive strain of B. subtilis (MTCC 121) as a model system to study the board-spectrum antibacterial activity against drug-resistant bacterial infections. Ficus Religeosa is an important medicinal tree species in the Moraceae family and is found throughout India. [18] Fig of Ficus religiosa plant is known for its antibacterial activity. [18,19] Interestingly, figs-derived Cdots were found to kill both the above drug-resistant Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria very effectively by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) and exhibit superior antibacterial activity than figs of the Ficus religiosa plant.

Fresh figs of Ficus religiosa were collected from the AMU campus and cleansed with deionized water. The figs were dehydrated in the oven at 70 °C for seven days. Then the dried figs were boiled in chloroform at 40 °C for 18 h, filtered with Whatman filter paper to remove the raw materials, and collected the extracted products. The extracted product was taken into a 250 mL beaker and then carbonized using a domestic microwave oven (Samsung microwave model-MC28H5033CK) at 600 W for 10 min. The as-synthesized products were purified using a dialysis membrane bag 1 kDa (benzoylated) for 12 h. The purified products were dried at 70 °C to get a viscous paste.

The morphology of as-synthesized Cdots was investigated using JEOL-JEM 2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Camera-AMT) operating at the maximum voltage of 200 kV. A Perkin Elmer LAMBDA-45 spectrophotometer was used to measure UV–vis absorption spectra. Photoluminesce spectra of Cdots were recorded using the HITACHI-F2500 photoluminescence spectrometer. FTIR spectrum was obtained by Perkin Elmer infra-red spectrometer. SEM analysis was performed using a JEOL JSM 7610F microscope. XPS experiment of Cdots was carried out with the help of ESCAMultilab 2000.

Antibacterial activity of Cdots was tested against the ampicillin-resistant Gram-negative strain of E. coli (DH5α) and tetracyclin-resistant Gram-positive strain of B. subtilis (MTCC 121). Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of Cdots were determined against the above bacterial organisms. MIC is the minimum concentration of any agent that inhibits the visible growth of an organism for a minimum of 12 h. MBC is the minimum concentration of any agent that needs to kill the bacteria over 24 h. Along with the determination of bacterial growth by measuring the optical density at 600 nm, CFU counting was also performed to estimate both MIC and MBC. In order to investigate the antibacterial activity of Cdots and its precursor (i.e., figs extracted product), the growth curve of E. coli (DH5α) was further performed for 12 h at 37 °C at their MIC concentration. The effective concentration of Cdots for bactericidal activity was calculated by determining the solubility of figs-derived Cdots in the aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of oxidative stress through NBT assay
ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) can be a major cause of the death of bacteria by oxidizing their cellular components. To understand the effects of Cdots on the production of ROS in E. coli (DH5α), NBT (Nitro blue tetrazolium) assay was performed. In the NBT assay, the level of ROS was measured by quantifying reduced NBT. Here, the overnight grew culture was incubated with Cdots (MIC and half MIC dose) in 0.2 mL of Hank's balanced salt solution (HBSS) in dark conditions at 37 °C for 30 min. After this, 0.1 mL of 10 mg/mL of NBT was added to the above solution and incubated for another 30 min at 37 °C in dark conditions. On completion of incubation, the reaction was stopped by adding 0.1 mL of 0.1 M HCl solution. Further, the bacterial pellet was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min, to which 0.5 mL of DMSO was added to obtain the reduced NBT (formazan). The amount of formazan formed was quantified by taking OD at 570 nm on a microplate reader. The untreated sample was considered a control.

Herein, a simple microwave-mediated synthesis of Cdots is reported using the extraction of figs of the Ficus religiosa tree. The synthesis protocol of figs-derived Cdots is demonstrated in the experimental section. After the purification, Cdots dispersed in ethanol was employed for further characterizations. Fig. 1a shows the absorption characteristics of figs-derived Cdots. The absorption peak at 276 nm is originated from the π-π∗ transition of the graphitic core in Cdots, whereas the weak band located at 409 nm is observed due to the surface states (or surface band) of Cdots [20]. The photophysical feature of Cdots dispersed in ethanol is demonstrated in Fig. 1b-d. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded over a wavelength range of 360 nm to 450 nm (Figure1b). The excitation-dependent PL of Cdots indicates no change in emission spectral shape (only change in emission intensity) up to the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. However, a slightly blue shift was noted for a higher excitation wavelength of 420 nm. This is due to different energy surface states present in Cdots [20]. Cdots exhibited maximum PL intensity at 673 nm with an excitation of 405 nm. The excitation spectrum of Cdots corresponding to the maximum emission (at 673 nm) is shown in Fig. 1c. Also, the quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 10.68% using Nile blue as a reference. To gain further insight into the photophysical behavior of Cdots, we recorded the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectrum (Fig. 1d). Interestingly, the exponential decay profile is fitted by a single exponential function, and the average lifetime (τav) corresponding to the decay is calculated to be 5.94 ns. The change in the absorption and PL spectrum was observed after the carbonization of extracted products of figs of Ficus religiosa, revealing the formation of Cdots (Fig. 1a, S1 & S2) [32].

The formation of Cdots was studied by TEM. The study divulged the formation of quasispherical Cdots with an average size of 7.4 ± 2.5 nm ( Fig. 2a). The information about the surface functionality of Cdots was obtained from Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum, which was performed on a KBr pellet (Fig. S3). The FTIR study indicates the presence of C–H, =C–H functional groups and oxygenated moieties such as C–O, –COO–, O–H, and CO. The absorption band at 3458 cm−1, 3016 cm−1, 2926 cm−1, 2856 cm−1, 1737 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, 1172 cm−1, and 732 cm−1 are assigned to the (νO-H), (ν = C-H), ( νas-C-H), (νsy-C-H), (ν CO), (δ- CH2), (C-CO-C stretching and bending) and (ρ-CH3) functional groups respectively. The change in the FTIR spectrum of Cdots compared to the FTIR spectrum of extracted products of figs of Ficus religiosa was noted (Fig. S3 & S4), which again confirms that the carbonization of extracted leads to the formation of Cdots. Further, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out to know the typical chemical bonding and elemental composition in the Cdots. The XPS full survey indicated that Cdots contain carbon, oxygen, and silicon elements (Fig. S5). The atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen and silicon are 83.39%, 10.60%, and 5.82%. The deconvoluted high-resolution C1 s spectrum implies the presence of C-C/CC (284.6 eV) and C-O/CO (centered at 285.3 eV) (Fig. 2b). Further, the deconvoluted high-resolution O1 s signal indicates the oxygenated functionality such as CO/Si-O-C (532 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (533.5 eV) present in Cdots (Fig. 2c). Notably, the deconvoluted peaks at 101.7 eV and 102.3 eV in the high-resolution Si2p signal signify the presence of Si-C/Si-O-C bonding in Cdots (Fig. 2d) [21].

Antibiotic resistance in bacterial species is a global challenge and trending research area [22]. Developing a potential non-metallic antibacterial agent through a sustainable method could be an alternative to antibiotics in primary and secondary care. Cdots show promising bactericidal activity against non-multidrug-resistant and multidrug-resistant bacteria and possess excellent biocompatibility [23]. Generally, antibacterial Cdots are synthesized following systematic strategies and chemical routes and then employed for antibacterial studies [5,23]. In this study, we employed biomass-derived Cdots for antibacterial activity against ampicillin-resistant recombinant strain E. coli (DH5α) and tetracycline-resistant B. subtilis (MTCC 121). The biomass-derived Cdots have many advantages as biomass is an excellent carbon source and contains medicinally important natural products [17]. Interestingly, figs-derived Cdots exhibited bactericidal activity against the antibiotic-resistant strains of bacteria. Fig. 3a demonstrates the time-dependent growth curve of E. coli (DH5α) in the presence and absence of figs-derived Cdots. The experiment was monitored at the optical density (OD) of 600 nm. The growth curve indicated significantly decreased bacterial growth with figs-derived Cdots. However, no apparent decrease in bacterial growth was observed in the control sample (i.e., the absence of Cdots) and with fig extracted products. The corresponding MIC and MBC values are given in Table 1. Notably, the MIC and MBC values of Cdots are comparable with the previously reported studies (Table S1) [24–30]. The fig-derived Cdots exhibit superior antibacterial activity compared to fig extracted products.

Recent studies suggested that Cdots can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) via cassette of single-electron reductions. [5,31] Therefore, we have carried out a ROS measurement experiment at MIC concentration of Cdots by NBT (nitro blue tetrazolium) assay to confirm ROS formation. Interestingly, figs-derived Cdots produced high amounts of formazan (blue color product), a reduced ROS product (Fig. 3b). On dropping the concentration of Cdots (i.e., half MIC), less formazan was formed than the MIC concentration of Cdots. The result confirmed the production of ROS, which leads to the oxidation of cellular components like proteins, membranes, and others, and eventually kills bacterial cells.

Further, we recorded SEM images of E. coli bacterial strain to study the morphological changes of bacteria upon treatment with figs-derived Cdots ( Fig. 4). The SEM image of control E. coli bacteria (untreated) demonstrated the regular, rod-shaped bacteria with rounded ends and intact cell walls (Fig. 4a). However, the SEM image revealed the deformed cell walls in the Cdots treated cells (Fig. 4b). Therefore, the above findings indicate that the bactericidal activity of figs-derived Cdots against drug-resistant bacteria is mediated through ROS generation.

Herein, we develop a rapid biomass-derived synthesis protocol of red emissive Cdots from Figs of the Ficus religiosa tree under microwave irradiation. The biomass contains medicinally important natural products and offers an excellent carbon source. The Cdots showed excellent antibacterial activity against drug-resistant bacterial strains like the Gram-negative strain of E. coli (DH5α) and the Gram-positive strain of B. subtilis (MTCC 121) at a relatively lower concentration. Notably, figs-derived Cdots exhibited superior bactericidal activity than the inherent antibacterial activity of figs extracted product. Further, the NBT assay experiment revealed that the Cdots can generate ROS, which might be one of the causes of bacterial cell death. Our finding might pave the way for developing a biomass-derived sustainable method of Cdots in searching for a novel antibacterial agent against drug-resistant bacterial strains.

MT synthesized and characterized Cdots and prepared manuscript. AS conducted all the antibacterial experiments and prepared the manuscript. NV helps in optical characterizations. SKS analyzed the data, reviewed and edited the manuscript. MPS conceptualized, designed the experiments, analyzed the data, wrote and reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MPS, MT, and NV acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University (AMU), for providing instrumental facilities. The authors acknowledge University Sophisticated Instrument Facility, AMU, Aligarh, and MNIT Jaipur for XPS facility. AS and SKS acknowledge the Department of Applied Sciences, IIIT Allahabad, for providing the facility. We thank Dr.Amaresh Kumar Sahoo (IIIT Allahabad) and. M. S. Hassan (IIT Delhi) for their help.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2022.104347.
